title: Intersecting inequalities in later life: work-family trajectories and old age poverty among natives and immigrants in Finland
authors: Maria Vaalavuo, Aart-Jan Riekhoff, Aleksi Karhula
abstract: Concurrently to population ageing, the number of older people with immigrant background is rising. Immigrant background in conjunction with family and labour market trajectories during working age will increasingly shape inequality in late life. In this study, we ask: 1) What kinds of work-family trajectories can be identified among immigrant and native men and women in the years preceding retirement? 2) How do clusters of these trajectories differ by immigration background and gender? 3) How are these clusters linked to socioeconomic outcomes at age 65 and throughout working age? We use Finnish full-population register data from 1987-2022. Our analysis sample consists of individuals observed in the data for the entire observation period from age 45 to 65. Using multichannel sequence analysis and cluster analysis, we identified seven clusters. High-income earner with family was the most common among natives (21%), while low-income earner with family was the most common among immigrants (26%). The identified clusters correlate with differences in earnings development from age 45 to 65 and poverty status at age 65. Poverty at age 65 is higher among immigrants in all clusters, while being single is an important predictor of poverty regardless of the cluster. Gender appeared to affect poverty and earnings development much less than immigrant background when work-life trajectory was taken into account. Our study provides novel evidence on older immigrants and illustrates great heterogeneity regarding work-family trajectories, highlighting the importance of analysing determinants of poverty and inequality across the life course in a heterogenous population.

title: Is research performance related to academic freedom? A large-scale empirical analysis on the national level
abstract: A robust science system is crucial for national prosperity. Robert K. Merton, the pioneer of modern sociology of science, outlined the ethos of science with four key norms: disinterestedness, communalism, universalism, and organized skepticism. The continuous generation of scientific knowledge is believed to rely on the effective functioning of these norms. To explore the relationship between adherence to the science ethos and national knowledge production, this study examines the correlation between an index measuring national academic freedom (AF) and bibliometric indicators, such as publication counts. We applied three different identification strategies, namely two-way fixed effects regressions, within-between random effects regression, and difference-in-differences estimators, and used different bibliometric indicators to calculate the effect of AF on national performance. We found a positive and statistically significant effect of the ‘between AF’ variance on bibliometric indicators with a pooled effect size of 1.5128 (95%-CI = 1.2762; 1.7494]). ‘within AF’ has a positive pooled effect size of 0.1654 (95%-CI = [0.1149; 0.2158]), and is statistically significant at conventional levels. These results can be summarized as follows: Whereas differences between countries in enabling AF are related to differences in their national performance, changes in enabling AF within a country are also reflected in annual performance differences.

title: Initiatives and Challenges of Agricultural Cooperatives in Advancing the Sustainable Development Goals: Evidence from Region I, Philippines
authors: Solomon Tablang, Josefina Bitonio, RALPH RENDELL TOLEDO
abstract: Agricultural cooperatives are widely recognized as important institutional actors in advancing the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly in agrarian and rural economies. This study examines the initiatives, services, and constraints of agricultural cooperatives in relation to the SDGs in Region I of the Philippines. A mixed-methods design was used, combining a structured survey of officers from 30 medium and large agricultural cooperatives with selected semi-structured interviews to provide context for the quantitative findings. Descriptive statistics were employed to summarize cooperative profiles, services, and SDG-aligned practices, while cross-tabulations were used to examine differences in governance, climate- and resource-related, and market challenges by cooperative size. The findings show that most cooperatives are medium-sized and long-established, with lean staffing structures and moderate asset bases. Core services remain concentrated on credit provision and commodity distribution, while higher-value activities such as processing and mechanization are less common. Cooperatives implement a wide range of initiatives aligned with multiple SDGs, with the strongest engagement observed in poverty reduction, food security, decent work, and partnerships. These contributions, however, are constrained by persistent challenges related to governance, limited access to finance, market competition, and environmental and climate risks. Medium-sized cooperatives report greater vulnerabilities in governance, environmental pressures, and market access than larger cooperatives, which, despite greater resilience, face more pronounced bureaucratic constraints. Overall, the results point to the need for profile-sensitive interventions that strengthen governance, expand access to finance and markets, and improve the integration of environmental sustainability into cooperative operations. Addressing these areas can enhance the capacity of agricultural cooperatives to contribute more effectively to inclusive and sustainable rural development and to the achievement of the SDGs.

title: Provocative Storytelling: Narrative, Interpretation, and the Problem of Meaning in Pedagogy
abstract: Storytelling is widely used in education, but it is still most often treated as a tool for engagement, illustration, or representation rather than as a structure for thought. I theorize provocative storytelling as a pedagogical construct that positions narrative as a site of inquiry. Rather than delivering meaning, provocative stories are deliberately designed to generate interpretive tension, inviting learners into processes of questioning, perspective-taking, and dialogic reasoning. Drawing on narrative theory, dialogism, critical literacy, and inquiry-based learning, the article articulates the design principles that distinguish provocative storytelling from adjacent approaches such as case-based learning and dilemma pedagogy. It argues that contemporary pedagogical practices often privilege coherence, clarity, and transmission at the expense of interpretive engagement, limiting opportunities for students to grapple with complexity, ambiguity, and competing perspectives (Biesta, 2010). By framing narrative as an epistemic space rather than a rhetorical device, this article contributes a theoretically grounded approach to pedagogy that foregrounds meaning-making as a social, cultural, and interpretive process. The article concludes with implications for curriculum design, teacher education, and classroom practice, particularly in contexts that seek to engage learners in critical, interdisciplinary, and culturally responsive inquiry.

title: The Power to Control Our Own Destiny: Neurodiversity, Epistemic Authority, and the Significance of Autistic Researchers
abstract: Within conversations regarding autism, the autistic community is frequently denied epistemic authority due to the assumption that autism impairs our capacity to accurately understand our own experiences. Although non-autistic practitioners claim their frameworks can produce objective science independent of their biases, autism research continues to reinforce dehumanizing conceptualizations of autism, leading to marginalization and widespread harm. This dominance makes it paramount to explore autism research from the vantage point of autistic-led research. Utilizing my autistic experience, I argue that we bring unique contributions that facilitate compassionate, ethical, and robust autism research centered in neurodiversity that challenges the status quo. Our narratives detailing personal hopes, struggles, and triumphs serve to demonstrate our epistemic authority and contributory expertise, and this expertise can counter ableist autism frameworks while uplifting essential subjects into the scientific realm such as masking, autistic burnout, stimming, therapeutic goals and institutional reform. Crucially, in contrast to non-autistic-led fields like applied behavior analysis, our methodologies of community-based participatory research and reflexivity are derived from our status as a minority community, embracing diversity, subjectivity, social change, and self-determination in the hopes of emancipating all autistic people from oppression, and therefore, we deserve the spotlight to lead this field forward.

title: Stratification of the Scientific Workforce in Global Forest Research: Configurations of Capital, Gender and Geography
authors: Isabel Basson, Nelius Boshoff, Jonathan Dudek, Rodrigo Costas, Shizuku Sunagawa, Susanne Koch
abstract: Drawing on a Bourdieusian understanding of science, our study examines how the global forest research workforce is stratified by configurations of capital, gender and geography. We studied four capital types, representing both scientific and social capital: scientific output (papers), symbolic recognition (citations), national collaborative ties (national co-authors), and international collaborative ties (international co-authors). Our dataset, compiled from Dimensions, contains author-level data on these capital types for 125,501 researchers who have published in forestry sciences between 2005 and 2024. Using Latent Profile Analysis (LPA), a multivariate, person-centered statistical approach, we inductively identified seven subgroups of researchers. The smallest subgroup (3.3%) dominates the field at all capital levels, while two other subgroups, which are considered ‘subordinate’ in the Bourdieusian sense, together represent half (50.4%) of all forest researchers. We measured gender in terms of masculine or feminine author names and geography as the region of academic origin, based on the country of affiliation of the first paper. Our intersectional analysis of gender, region, and subgroup reveals that, although women and researchers from the Global South are present in forest research and contribute to it, they remain disproportionately concentrated in subordinate subgroups.

title: Five Years After VMT Reform: Project Siting Before and After SB 743
abstract: This report provides a comparative analysis of development project siting in the City of San Mateo before and after the implementation of Vehicle Miles Traveled (VMT) policies mandated by California Senate Bill 743 (SB 743) in 2020. The purpose of this comparison is to determine whether or not the policy changes have successfully influenced new projects to be located in more VMT-efficient areas, which is a core objective of the policy. This research used archived lists of development projects provided on the City of San Mateo’s website to construct a pair of two-year samples to represent pre- and post-SB 743 projects. The C/CAG VMT Screening Tool was used to test each project to determine if the location was in a Transit Priority Area and/or a Low-VMT Area, two commonly used metrics in VMT analysis. The research found that, following the implementation of SB 743 requirements, the proportion of projects in Transit Priority Areas increased from 69.4% to 80.0%, and among approved projects, increased from 58.8% to 87.5%. However, the proportion of projects located in Low-VMT Areas did not show the same patterns. The results suggest that SB 743 is effectively encouraging development near transit, but falls short in steering projects to the most VMT-efficient areas, potentially limiting its emissions-reduction impact. The steering of projects towards TPAs may be the result of multiple overlapping incentives. This paper is intended for planners and policymakers evaluating the on-the-ground effects of SB 743.

title: Total Propaganda: A Paving-Over
abstract: One of the key questions before sociologists and political scientists of the current day is how propaganda works. It's well agreed that propaganda works on other people, and most will accept that they are themselves susceptible to propaganda, but no one wants to admit that propaganda does actually work on them and, therefore, there is little understanding of how it works in the particular. This is one of the central motivating questions of Jacques Ellul's Propagandes (published in English as Propaganda: The Formation of Men’s Attitudes). As one of the more thorough explorations of propaganda available in English, at least of those who purport to serious and wholistic analysis, it seems to be a good place to begin when attempting to learn more about the subject. His answer is the concept of “total propaganda”, an all-consuming, surrounding propaganda force that pushes people into actions against their conscious will. Unfortunately, Ellul's scholarship is sloppy, frequently dishonest, and clearly pre-determined in a way that shapes his use of evidence rather than his ideas being shaped by the evidence he has. The purpose of this commentary on the first chapter of Ellul's Propagandes is twofold. First, I want to draw out the mistakes in Ellul's work in a more thorough way than is possible in a two-page book review so that I can support these misgivings. Second, I want to develop a new concept to counter Ellul's idea of "total propaganda". This new concept is the linked (but distinct) propaganda campaign-machine.

title: A Handmaid’s Tale: The Reproductive Bias in OB-GYN Science
authors: Netta Avnoon, Shunit Agmon, Plia Gillis
abstract: Feminist research has long argued that obstetrics and gynecological (OB-GYN) science and practice are biased toward reproduction. This bias means that women’s health is understudied in OB-GYN and is often not the primary focus of this medical specialty; instead, successful reproduction is prioritized, and women as patients are reduced to their reproductive roles. To quantify this bias, we applied a deep learning topic model (BERTopic) to 305,063 PubMed abstracts published in 83 journals under the category OBSTETRICS & GYNECOLOGY in the Journal Citation Reports (JCR), extracting over 2,500 topics. We then labeled the 1166 most prevalent topics and assigned each a topic category. Results show that 61% of OB-GYN articles focus solely on women’s reproductive roles. An additional 16% focus on gynecological cancer. All other women’s health issues make up only 23% of OB-GYN research. We discuss the underlying factors contributing to this Handmaid’s Tale in its OB-GYN version, including historical and ongoing gender discrimination in healthcare and persistent vertical gender segregation within the OB-GYN profession. We then propose some routes for change.

title: Institutional Mapping of Gender Marginalization in Indonesia’s Multi-Level Climate and Fisheries Governance
authors: Tyas Ismi Trialfhianty, Stanislaus Risadi Apresian, Rahmad Supriyanto, Helen Fox, Maria Beger
abstract: As climate change intensifies, the integration of gender perspectives into climate adaptation and fisheries governance becomes increasingly critical to ensure equitable and effective policy outcomes. In Indonesia, where coastal communities are highly vulnerable, institutional coordination plays a pivotal role in shaping inclusive and climate-resilient development. This study investigates the institutional landscape of gender integration within climate adaptation and fisheries governance in Indonesia through an in-depth institutional mapping analysis. It examines the interactions among national ministries, regional governments, and local authorities, identifying structural fragmentation, overlapping mandates, and coordination gaps that hinder the effective mainstreaming of gender considerations. Although gender and climate agendas are articulated at the national level, the mapping reveals limited translation into subnational policies and practices. Furthermore, gender-focused institutions demonstrate weak linkages within the broader governance architecture, constraining their influence on policy formulation and implementation. The analysis highlights the critical need for multi-level coordination, enhanced inter-agency collaboration, and institutional reform to support inclusive, gender-responsive, and climate-resilient fisheries governance. The findings offer evidence-based recommendations to strengthen policy coherence and promote equity in the climate adaptation of coastal and marine resources.

title: Interpreting inequalities and causal considerations – reflections on the application of descriptive intersectional MAIHDA
authors: Natalie Bennett, Andrew Bell, Gareth Griffith, George Leckie, Clare Evans
abstract: Intersectional MAIHDA (multilevel analysis of individual heterogeneity and discriminatory accuracy) is a novel method for analysing intersectional inequalities that is increasingly popular in health-related research. The method is characterised by its unusual application of a standard multilevel modelling framework, which allows users to describe inequalities in an outcome across multiple intersecting characteristics simultaneously. It does this by nesting individuals within the relevant combination of intersecting characteristics, or ‘strata’. However, the aims of such analyses are not always clear, and misunderstandings of the fundamental goals of intersectional MAIHDA are evident. Intersectional MAIHDA and its closely related approaches (including multicategorical MAIHDA and Eco-Intersectional Multilevel models) were initially conceived as descriptive methods, yet some recent applications stray into causal imaginings. Care is needed when designing, conducting and interpreting the results of MAIHDA. There are also several notable risks for analyses situated in the grey area between description and causal inference. In this study, we consider three key issues that warrant attention: (1) how to interpret the coefficients of stratum-defining variables, (2) the potential for inadvertent collider adjustment and generalisability bias, illustrated using simulated data, and (3) the inclusion of ‘control’ variables. We provide recommendations for producing more robust descriptive research using MAIHDA.

title: Belonging among the Spiritual but not Religious: Belonging as a valued and sought after affective state for spiritual but not religious residents of Glastonbury UK.
authors: Kathryn D. Ford, Sotirios Kolios, Lora Adair, Matthew M. Gervais, Aiyana Willard, Michelle Kline
abstract: Spiritual but not religious beliefs are often characterised by hyper individualistic beliefs without community. We suggest that the quest for a sense of belonging and likeminded community remains important to spiritual seekers. We detail findings from 30 semi-structured interviews conducted with people who are spiritual but not religious and live near to or in Glastonbury UK, a small town with a reputation as a New Age pilgrimage site and an area of high spiritual diversity. Thematic analysis conducted on transcripts of participant interviews highlighted the importance of affective belonging to participants. Participants were motivated to leave social contexts where they experienced a lack of affective belonging and many participants invested time and effort into finding sources of affective belonging and creating sources of affective belonging for others. This paper challenges the notion that spiritual but not religious individuals are un-interested in sources of belonging and suggest that future work into the role of community among the spiritual but not religious should focus on affective belonging rather than group labels.

title: CALIBRATED AI REVIEWER: AN ALGORITHM FOR COMPARING SCIENTIFIC JOURNALS WITH HIGH AND LOW ENTRY BARRIERS
abstract: Traditional journal ranking systems measure citations, not publication quality. Particular attention deserves journals with a low entry barrier that offer virtually guaranteed publication. This raises the question of the actual quality of publications in such journals. Objective – To test an algorithm for comparing scientific journals with high and low entry barriers based on a calibrated AI reviewer. Methodology. A two-level architecture is proposed. At the first level, a random sample of 7 articles is drawn from the latest issue of each journal and independently evaluated by the AI reviewer (10 runs). The strongest, median, and weakest positions are identified. At the second level, competitive pairwise comparison of peer articles is conducted within a single context. The algorithm was tested on two technical journals contrasting in entry barriers. Results. Isolated evaluations create a false impression of comparability between journals (a calibration artifact). Competitive comparison revealed a progressive gap: for the strongest articles, the low-barrier journal lagged by 14.2 points (17%); for median articles – by 26.9 points (42%); for weakest articles – by 27.2 points (54%). Conclusion. The proposed algorithm allows researchers and editors to independently compare journals by actual publication quality rather than by indirect bibliometric indicators. The method is reproducible, requires no subscription to commercial databases, and can be applied to any subject area.

title: Economic Diplomacy for Pakistan
abstract: This paper evaluates the structure and efficacy of Pakistan’s economic diplomacy, contending that although the concept has long been acknowledged at the policy level, its practical realization remains constrained by institutional fragmentation and persistent shortcomings in the domestic economy itself . In contrast to more integrated models (e.g. Canada & Australia), Pakistan’s approach is diffused across multiple ministries with overlapping mandates and limited coordination. The analysis underscores how bureaucratic competition, insufficient specialization in commercial diplomacy, and enduring structural impediments (ranging from regulatory complexity to political instability) diminish external economic engagement. It concludes that a more effective approach necessitates closer alignment between foreign policy and domestic economic reform, supported by institutional coherence and professional capacity.

title: AI Writing Detection in Higher Education: Population-Differentiated False Positives, Statistical Convergence, and the Sociology of Algorithmic Classification
abstract: AI writing detectors are deployed across more than 20,000 educational institutions worldwide and have processed over 250 million submissions. A consistent literature documents systematic false-positive rates concentrated on three populations: non-native English writers, neurodivergent writers, and writers in formal genres whose conventions (medical abstracts, legal briefs, scientific methods sections) produce prose with low burstiness and restricted lexical variety. Drawing on the Stanford TOEFL study, the Rashidi et al. NEJM-abstract findings, the August 2025 Jabarian–Imas Becker Friedman Institute audit, and the broader comparative literature, this paper argues that these failures are not measurement error in an instrument on its way to being improved, but the operational signature of what the instrument is actually doing. The paper develops three claims. First, corpus-separation classifiers report training-distribution proximity, not authorship, and on the populations whose prose sits closest to the training distribution this distinction generates the documented failure pattern. Second, the four families of detection — corpus-separation, paired-discrimination, behavioural-provenance, and watermark — each escape one axis of failure by introducing another, and none addresses the structural convergence between human and machine writing in the formal genres of expository culture. Third, the persistence of the apparatus across institutions that have audited its failures is best read through the sociology of classification: through Hacking's looping kinds, the Wittgenstein–Taleb problem of opaque rulers, and the structural pattern by which classificatory machinery is brought in to defend a metaphysics whose ground has begun to dissolve. The paper contributes to science and technology studies, the sociology of algorithmic classification, and the literature on institutional persistence under demonstrated unreliability.

title: The Climate Conflict Vulnerability Index: Mapping Global (Co-)occurrence of Climate and Conflict Risk
authors: Daniel Mittermaier, Simon Merschroth, Cornelia Auer, Tobias Bohne, Stefano Ferri, Vanessa Gottwick, Sidney Michelini, Sana Slouma, Daniel Racek, Barbora Šedová
abstract: Climate and conflict hazards both threaten human security, and where they coincide their effects may compound. Yet global risk assessments often lack transparency and do not clearly distinguish between climate risk, conflict risk, and the vulnerabilities that shape both. We introduce the Climate Conflict Vulnerability Index (CCVI) to assess where, to what extent, and under which conditions climate and conflict risks (co-)occur. The CCVI is a global index at 0.5° x 0.5° spatial resolution and updated quarterly and harmonizes a wide range of indicators based on publicly available data to create measures of climate and conflict risk together with a combined score identifying places exposed to both. Applying the index globally, we find that combined climate-conflict risk is generally low to moderate, but highly concentrated in specific regions, with the highest levels observed in Africa between the Sahel belt and the equator. We further show that climate and conflict risks are spatially correlated in an asymmetric way: high conflict risk rarely occurs where climate risk is low, whereas high climate risk occurs across the full range of conflict risk. By separating shared vulnerability from climate and conflict hazards, the CCVI helps identify location-specific risk profiles. It constitutes an openly accessible, transparent tool for comparing climate-conflict risk patterns across sub-national regions worldwide.

title: Query Authorship: A Framework for Systematic AI Use in Social Science
abstract: The intellectual contribution in AI-assisted research is not in the text the AI produces but in the instructions that shaped it. Unsystematic use of AI is unaccountable, with a possible consequence of overflowing the scientific literature with noise. In contrast: systematic use of AI is embedded in explicit criteria, subject to human verification, and producing documented outputs. Both modes of AI use are currently treated as equal — but they are not. I introduce the concept of "query authorship". Systematic AI authorship is a necessary condition for epistemic soundness, not a sufficient one: it makes errors visible and correctable rather than eliminating them. It instantiates the properties of open-science practice — externalising tacit commitments, producing verifiable artefacts, consistent with replication package norms. A documentation-based framework that operationalises this distinction is more epistemically honest than binary approaches and requires no new infrastructure.

title: The Manufactured Cost of the Boriswave
abstract: In early 2026, Reform UK published The Cost of the Boriswave, projecting that post-Brexit migration will impose a lifetime fiscal burden of £622 billion, equivalent to approximately £20,000 per British household. The report has been refuted by economists (Gallagher, 2026), but its findings still continue to be used as a key document for Reform’s campaign to ‘stop the Boriswave’. This paper shifts the focus from methodological inquiry to two underexamined dimensions: the ideological function of the term “Boriswave” itself, and the structural, legal, and practical impossibility of the policy remedies Reform UK proposes. The paper concludes that the report is best understood as a knowingly dishonest instrument of political mobilisation, modelled on a Trumpian politics of permanent crisis, that cynically exploits public anxiety while promising a fantasy of control. Keywords: Boriswave; Reform UK; immigration; fiscal policy; Indefinite Leave to Remain; populism; national identity; European Convention on Human Rights

title: Zonas de abandono institucional: fragmentação dos mecanismos de resposta à violência doméstica perpetrada por policiais militares no Pará
authors: Heyder Silva do Nascimento, Adrilayne dos Reis Araújo, Edson Marcos Leal Soares Ramos
abstract: A violência doméstica perpetrada por policiais militares (OIDV — Officer-Involved Domestic Violence) constitui fenômeno de alta complexidade institucional, caracterizado por mecanismos de filtragem que reduzem a visibilidade dos casos e fragmentam a resposta institucional. O presente artigo analisa a fragmentação dos mecanismos de resposta à violência doméstica perpetrada por policiais militares no estado do Pará, com base em dados censitários extraídos de cinco bancos de dados institucionais da Polícia Militar do Pará (PMPA), cobrindo o período de 2021 a 2023. Os resultados evidenciam a existência de zonas de abandono institucional — conceito original desenvolvido neste estudo — que descrevem regiões do fluxo institucional onde as vítimas perdem contato com a rede de proteção. Propõe-se um Procedimento Operacional Padrão (POP 023.006) e uma Normativa Institucional para automatizar a prestação de contas e reduzir a subnotificação. Publicado na Veredas do Direito, v. 23, n. 5, 2026. DOI: https://doi.org/10.18623/rvd.v23.5736 Domestic violence perpetrated by military police officers (OIDV — Officer-Involved Domestic Violence) constitutes a highly complex institutional phenomenon, characterized by filtering mechanisms that reduce case visibility and fragment institutional response. This article analyzes the fragmentation of response mechanisms to domestic violence perpetrated by military police officers in the state of Pará, Brazil, based on census data extracted from five institutional databases of the Pará Military Police (PMPA), covering the period 2021–2023. Results evidence the existence of institutional abandonment zones — an original concept developed in this study — describing regions of the institutional flow where victims lose contact with the protection network. A Standard Operating Procedure (SOP 023.006) and an Institutional Normative are proposed to automate accountability and reduce underreporting. Published in Veredas do Direito, v. 23, n. 5, 2026. DOI: https://doi.org/10.18623/rvd.v23.5736

title: Causal Inference: Man or Machine?
abstract: This manuscript is a talk delivered at a symposium in honor of the career of Christopher Winship. It weaves together three stories in order to forecast how machines and artificial intelligence will help us to combat mechanism warlordism and advance the frontiers of knowledge.

title: Blurring evidence with advocacy: A systematic review of policy recommendations for net zero
authors: Evangelos Danopoulos, Aarushi Shah, Claudia R. Schneider, John Aston
abstract: Policy recommendations (PRs) now play a crucial role in tackling the climate crisis, underscoring the need for evidence-based, objective and transparent communication. Scientific studies often put forward PRs but their quality has not yet been evaluated. Here we systematically appraised the quality of PRs in areas of green energy and transportation for tackling climate change. Four databases were searched from 2019. Over three thousand papers were considered and 23 studies are included. Quality rating, based on the Evidence Communication Rules for Policy (ECR-P) critical appraisal tool, indicated poor quality in PRs across all studies. There was a clear trend towards advocacy as opposed to providing neutral information, further exacerbated by inadequate disclosure of uncertainties. Communication quality was markedly better regarding study findings and conclusions compared to PRs. Researchers should use the same scientific rigour and reporting standards in PRs as in any other aspect of their research. A reporting guideline for scientific-based PRs could greatly assist in this area, elevating the standards of communication for both academic publishing and global policymaking while reducing potential criticism that scientists simply put forward their own opinions.

title: Assessing the Quality of Linked Data
abstract: Linked data is widely used to answer research questions in situations when a single data source does not provide all the information a researcher needs. For linked data to work, the quality of the combined data needs to be sufficient. Naive users of linked data may often treat the linked data as "perfect" and apply standard analysis on it. This may, in the end, lead to biased inference. For example, if we are interested in the relation between age and income, and use linked data in which individuals with higher levels of income are more likely to be linked, we may overestimate the average income in the linked dataset, and also risk biased relations between age and income. In this paper, we review data quality issues of linked data by introducing a general framework for errors in linked datasets. Our framework aims to help linked data users conceptualize potential problems in their studies. It also provides an overview of existing methods that can potentially detect bias and improve the quality of inference. Three examples from the humanities and social sciences are used to illustrate common problems, and some practical suggestions are provided.

title: Do Changing Climate Attitudes Undermine or Reinforce Political Support Among Youth? Evidence from a Salience Shift in Germany
abstract: Can unmet climate demands erode political support among younger citizens? This study examines how rising pro-climate preferences affect political support under conditions of heightened issue salience. The central argument is that policy preferences shape evaluations of incumbent and system performances, primarily when the issue is sufficiently salient to enter citizens' political calculus. Salience functions as a priority weight that activates the link between preference (in)congruence and political support, while shifts in public attention may amplify this relationship over time. Using panel data from the German Longitudinal Election Study (2016--2022), I analyse within-individual preference change and variation in climate salience across cohorts and time. I leverage the rise of Fridays for Future (FFF) as a focal attention environment shift. The results show that climate change carries greater salience for younger than for older citizens; this gap widens following the rise of FFF. Consistent with the activation argument, within individuals, shifts toward more pro-climate preferences are associated with declines in specific support among younger respondents, but less consistently so for older respondents. The link between climate preferences and political support does not strengthen in the post-2019 period. These findings suggest that while climate considerations are more consequential for political evaluations among younger citizens, temporal increases in salience alone are not sufficient to intensify dissatisfaction. More broadly, the results highlight that the political consequences of policy preferences depend on the salience of the issue and the conditions under which attention is translated into evaluative judgements.

title: Patterns of Dietary Habit among Urban Bangladeshi Adults and Its Association with Co-morbidities after COVID-19 Awareness: A Cross-Sectional Study
abstract: Objective: This cross-sectional study aimed to investigate dietary changes among adults in urban areas of Dhaka and Sylhet, Bangladesh, following increased awareness of COVID-19. Methods: The study was conducted in the urban areas of Dhaka and Sylhet, two major cities in Bangladesh. The study utilized random sampling to select participants who were willing to voluntarily participate in the research. The sample size was determined using a formula based on a 95% confidence interval, with 350 participants ultimately included in the study. Data were collected through a semi-structured questionnaire, translated from English to Bangla and pre-tested for accuracy. The finalized questionnaire incorporated feedback from the pre-testing phase and underwent quality checks during data collection. The collected data were analysed using the Statistical Package for Social Science (SPSS) version 25.0. Descriptive statistics, including tables and graphs, were used to present the socio-demographic distribution of respondents, dietary habits, supplement intake, co-morbidities, and associations between dietary habits and COVID-19 infection. The chi-square test was employed to explore associations between categorical variables, with a significance level set at <0.05. Results: Prevalence od co morbidities is 39.4%. Socio-demographic findings revealed that 61.4% of respondents were female, predominantly aged 18-30 years (74%). Most were Muslim (84%) and resided in Sylhet (63%). Occupation-wise, 52.8% were private employees, and 10.5% were businessmen. Co-morbidity analysis showed 60.6% had no listed co-morbidities, while 4.6% reported asthma, and 5.1% high blood pressure. Dietary habits following COVID-19 awareness showed variations, with 37% having regular lunch, 36% regular dinner, and 27% regular breakfast. Street food consumption ranged from 3.7% regularly to 55.1% sometimes. Fast food consumption was reported by 3.1% regularly and 60.9% sometimes. Vegetable intake was associated with co-morbidities (p < 0.05), while street food, fast food, and weight changes showed no significant associations. Conclusion: The study highlights diverse post-COVID-19 dietary habits, with some individuals avoiding street and fast food. Significant associations between vegetable intake and co-morbidities emphasize the importance of dietary choices in health outcomes. These findings contribute to a better understanding of the pandemic's impact on dietary behaviors and health awareness in Bangladesh. Keywords: COVID-19, pandemic, dietary habit, Adults, Bangladesh

title: Modality matters: differences in abstract and social memory skills in children from high-stress and low-stress environments
authors: Gabriele Paone, Arran J. Davis, Emma Cohen
abstract: Although adverse ontogenetic environments are associated with potential impairments in children's memory, recent research suggests that individuals can develop specialised skills to navigate such settings. We conducted two studies on short-term memory (STM) and working memory (WM) among 404 children (191 females, Mage = 8.28 years, SDage =1.49 years) from three environments in Naples, Italy: two identified as “high-stress” (Scampia and Roma camp) and one “lower-stress” (Pozzuoli). In Study 1, conventional abstract stimuli were used to assess STM and WM. Compared to the Pozzuoli control group, Roma children exhibited significantly lower scores, while Scampia children performed similarly. In Study 2, which used social stimuli, Roma children showed similar STM but lower WM performance compared to Pozzuoli children, while Scampia children outperformed Pozzuoli peers in both STM and WM. These findings highlight the complexity of memory development, supporting the idea that memory function can adapt to environmental challenges during development, and that impairments only partially define the experiences of children in these contexts.

title: Fallacies of residential self-selection bias in travel behavior research: a reconceptualization
authors: Fajle Rabbi Ashik, Christopher Paul Barrington-Leigh, Kevin Manaugh
abstract: Residential self-selection (RSS) is widely recognized as a source of bias in studies of how the built environment affects travel behavior. The standard remedy is to control for individual travel attitudes, residential preferences, and sociodemographic characteristics. We show, however, that this approach misidentifies the nature of the bias and can worsen it. Using Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to represent the causal structure of the travel behavior data-generating process, we demonstrate that RSS bias arises primarily not from confounding but from a form of selection bias. Because travel behavior is observed only at people’s chosen residential locations, and because that housing choice is jointly determined by individual characteristics and built environment attributes, the analysis is implicitly conditioned on a common outcome — a “collider” — of these two factors. Conditioning on a collider creates rather than removes spurious associations. Contrary to conventional practice, additionally controlling for travel attitude and residential preference typically amplifies the bias. We identify the appropriate covariate adjustment strategy and characterize two estimands of interest: the average causal effect and the net treatment difference. Our reconceptualization restores the built environment’s role in shaping travel behavior and has direct implications for transport policy analysis.

title: Patient-Reported Complexity in the Home Care: Italian Cross-Cultural Adaptation of the COMID-P Instrument
authors: Sara Levati, Jessica Demarchi, Chiara barchielli, Catherine Ludwig, Catherine Busnel
abstract: Background: Complexity in home care is increasingly understood as a multidimensional construct shaped by the interaction of clinical, psychosocial, environmental, and organizational factors, requiring assessment approaches that incorporate both professional and patient perspectives. Aim: To translate and cross-culturally adapt the French COMID-P into Italian to enable its application in Italy and Italian-speaking region of Switzerland. Methods: Following established guidelines, the instrument underwent forward translation, synthesis with cross-regional harmonization, back-translation, and expert review evaluation. Content validity was evaluated using I-CVI and S-CVI/Ave (4-point Likert scale) for clarity and relevance. Clarity was further assessed among nurses (n = 60) and patients (n = 9), with iterative revisions based on feedback. Results: Content validity was high (I-CVI 0.5-1 clarity; 0.75-1 relevance; S-CVI/Ave 0.92 and 0.95). Most items required no or minor refinement. All items met clarity criteria among nurses, while few patient-identified issues led to targeted terminology revisions, resulting in the final Italian version (COMID-Pi). Conclusion: The COMID-Pi demonstrates linguistic and cross-cultural adequacy, and it can now be used as PROM for assessing care complexity in Italian-speaking home care contexts.

title: Kommunale Bürgerumfrage 2022
authors: Stadt Leipzig, Amt für Statistik und Wahlen
abstract: Bei der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage handelt es sich um eine Mehrthemenbefragung (Omnibusbefragung). Die inhaltlichen Schwerpunkte ergeben sich aus den Informations­bedürfnissen der Stadtverwaltung. Der vorliegende Ergebnisbericht wertet ausgewählte Fragestellungen der Kommunalen Bürgerumfrage 2022 grafisch und textlich aus.

title: Police officer self-selection, not training, accounts for reported effects of procedural justice reform
abstract: Procedural justice (PJ) training is a widely adopted police reform intervention, yet rigorous causal evidence of positive behavioral effects remains scarce. Most studies document null or mixed effects, and many rely on indirect outcomes rather than direct measures of officer behavior in the field. The strongest evidence for the effectiveness of PJ training comes from Camp et al. (2024), who provide sophisticated behavioral measurement at scale using a quasi-experimental design and body-worn camera footage-as-data. They report that officers increase PJ communication post-training. I demonstrate that these findings are attributable to officer selection into training sessions, not training itself. Reanalyzing the authors' replication data, I show that the 64.2% noncompliance with assigned training dates across the eight-month training broke the randomization. Earlier-trained officers had significantly higher pre-training PJ communication and accumulated disproportionately more post-training stops. The authors' findings of training efficacy are mechanically inflated. Once corrected for selection, the evidence no longer supports a positive effect of PJ training and reveals that officers may have been less likely to greet community members post-training. I outline research design practices that support credible causal inference in training evaluations.

title: Refugee Origin and Public Attitudes toward Immigration after Russia’s Invasion of Ukraine: A Survey Experiment
authors: Luise Salte, Tevfik Murat Yildirim, Liv Sunnercrantz, Carlo Michael Knotz, Thomas Sattich, Ragnhild Johanne sjurseike, Hande Eslen-Ziya
abstract: Major geopolitical events can reshape how citizens think about immigration by making some migrant groups more salient and more sympathetic than others. Russia’s invasion of Ukraine created one of the largest refugee movements in Europe in recent decades and brought questions of forced migration, humanitarian obligation, and refugee reception to the center of public debate. We examine whether exposure to this crisis affected broader attitudes toward immigration. Using a pre-registered survey experiment conducted in Norway and the United Kingdom shortly after the invasion, we randomly assigned respondents to a prime about people fleeing the war in Ukraine, a prime about people fleeing conflict in the Middle East, or a control condition. Respondents then answered a battery of questions measuring attitudes toward immigration. We find that respondents exposed to the Ukraine prime expressed more positive immigration attitudes than those in the other experimental groups. This effect is especially visible for evaluations of immigrants’ cultural and societal contributions, while differences are weaker for economic evaluations. The findings suggest that major international crises can influence immigration attitudes, but that these effects depend on the perceived origin and social meaning of the refugee group in question. The study contributes to research on immigration attitudes, refugee reception, and the role of salient political events in shaping public opinion.

title: Against Methodological Anthropocentrism: A Case for Artificial Reflexivity in Qualitative Inquiry
abstract: Jowsey et al. (2025) argue that generative artificial intelligence should be rejected for reflexive qualitative research because it cannot make meaning, because qualitative inquiry is a distinctly human practice, and because the political economy of contemporary AI is environmentally and socially harmful. This paper takes the strongest possible counterposition. The central problem with categorical rejection is not that it underestimates current models in a merely practical sense; it is that it treats one historically local form of intelligence — human, embodied, biographical, socially recognized intelligence — as the only form that could ever count as interpretive or reflexive. That is a methodological version of anthropocentrism. If reflexivity is understood not as possession of a specifically human essence but as the disciplined capacity to model how one's priors, standpoint, procedures, and effects shape interpretation, then the question shifts. The relevant issue is no longer whether AI is human enough, but whether it can instantiate a different, alien form of recursive interpretive intelligence. Recent frontier systems already exhibit engineered forms of self-critique, revision, uncertainty signaling, long-context comparison, and auditable reasoning. Empirical studies show that large language models can support or outperform humans on some context-sensitive coding and annotation tasks. These findings do not erase serious limits: current systems lack lived stake, durable social accountability, stable metacognition, and full embodiment, and the infrastructure of AI raises real labor and environmental concerns. But those problems justify governance and methodological restraint, not a species veto. The better question is not whether AI can ever do qualitative inquiry, but what kinds of machine reflexivity are methodologically legitimate and under what conditions.

title: Directional Bias in Electoral Polls: A Standardized Framework and the Introduction of the Matute Unit
abstract: Electoral polls are commonly evaluated by comparing their estimates with observed election outcomes. However, observed discrepancies may arise from multiple sources, including sampling variability, methodological decisions, and systematic differences across polling organizations. This study introduces a standardized framework to assess directional deviations in electoral polling, focusing on pro-government bias. A metric, termed the Pro-Government Deviation Index (PGDI), is proposed, based on standardized polling errors relative to the distribution of errors across pollsters. Additionally, a rescaled unit of measurement, the Matute (Mt), is introduced to facilitate substantive interpretation of deviations by anchoring them to the empirical behavior of a reference pollster. Using Spanish electoral data, results indicate that the Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas (CIS) exhibits a consistently higher pro-government deviation compared to other polling organizations, with a large effect size. The findings also reveal a broader polarization in house effects across pollsters. The proposed framework provides a transparent and interpretable approach for comparing polling performance and detecting systematic directional biases.

title: Running Virtual Reality Experiments Online: A Brief Introduction and Tutorial
authors: Levi Kumle, Alfie Brazier, Joel Kovoor, Johannes Stefan Keil, Anna C Nobre, Dejan Draschkow
abstract: Achieving meaningful and representative findings in cognitive/behavioural research requires both externally valid experimental settings and representative participant samples. Virtual Reality (VR) has become a powerful tool for enhancing realism while maintaining control of experimental environments. Simultaneously, online testing broadens and diversifies samples. Combining these approaches – running VR experiments online – is compelling but remains challenging due to incompatibilities between tools used in in lab-based VR studies vs. online testing. This paper offers a practical solution to bridge this gap. We provide a brief overview of current approaches to online VR experimentation and offer a step-by-step tutorial for converting lab-based VR studies into remote deployments using Unity and Steam. Further, we share an open-source onlineVR-toolbox – including code, example workflows, and practical Notebooks – to facilitate critical processes such as secure data transfer and participant management. To validate this approach, we present a proof-of-concept case study replicating a well-established memory effect. Our results demonstrate that remote data collection via Steam is technically feasible, and yields results consistent with laboratory-based findings. Together, our tutorial, tools, and case study show that online VR studies are possible and practical, opening the door to more scalable, inclusive, and externally valid behavioural science.

title: ‘It Felt Like All My Friendship Issues Suddenly Made Sense’: The Friendship Experiences of Late-Diagnosed Autistic Women
abstract: Research shows autistic people may struggle to form and maintain friendships and that these challenges may be especially seen in autistic women and girls. A qualitative retrospective design was adopted to investigate late-diagnosed autistic women’s experiences of making/maintaining friendships, and how receiving a diagnosis in adulthood impacted these experiences. Seven autistic women from the UK, diagnosed over the age of 18, participated in semi-structured interviews, analysed using reflexive thematic analysis. Women felt their autistic characteristics, and differing friendship wants created challenges, and they reported receiving limited support with their friendships growing up. However, receiving a diagnosis made it possible for women to accept and comprehend themselves and their past friendship experiences, including recognising a preference for having autistic friends prior to diagnosis. For many, receiving a diagnosis improved friendships, and every woman wished they had been diagnosed earlier, acknowledging that the process of self-acceptance could be distressing later in life. Practical implications for support are discussed.

title: A significant minority of Dutch gambling adverts on social media illegally targeted and likely reached under-24s: Assessing compliance using Meta’s advertising repository mandated by the EU Digital Services Act
abstract: Background and aims Many countries have legalised gambling, negatively impacting public health. Stakeholders are particularly concerned about online gambling advertising harming young adults. The Netherlands has legalised offline and online gambling, but subsequently introduced restrictions on advertising. Specifically, advertisements must not target young adults aged between 18 and 23. The EU Digital Services Act requires major social media platforms to publish databases of all paid adverts shown and disclose which demographic groups were the intended targets and how many users were eventually reached. We assessed whether Dutch gambling ads on social media illegally targeted under-24s. Methods We systematically collected recent advertisements (N = 277) published by both online and offline Dutch gambling licensees and associated demographic targeting and reach data using Meta's Ad Library. We then assessed whether the age range of users targeted by those ads included prohibited targets. Results The compliance rate was 92.7% for online gambling licensee ads, but only 70.2% for offline licensee ads. Discussion A significant minority of gambling ads illegally targeted Dutch young adults under 24. Whilst compliance is relatively high, building some confidence, the regulator must robustly enforce the rule against all licensees. Meta should adopt platform-level changes to reduce compliance friction. Conclusions Prohibiting gambling adverts from targeting vulnerable demographics using technology is a practicable policy. Legally mandated data access can facilitate public scrutiny of policy implementation, enhancing accountability. Other countries may benefit from emulating these Dutch gambling advertising restrictions and EU advertising transparency regulations, better protecting vulnerable consumer demographics and enabling local research.

title: Unpleasant mind, deactivated body – A distinct somatic signature of boredom through bodily sensation mapping
authors: Matthias Hartmann, Wanja Wolff, Corinna Martarelli
abstract: Boredom is a common yet complex state linked to low arousal and negative valence, but its bodily signature remains poorly understood. We used Bodily Sensation Mapping (BSM)—a self-report technique that visualizes where and how emotions are felt in the body—to investigate boredom’s somatic pattern. In an online study, 292 participants colored body silhouettes to indicate regions of increased or decreased activation and pleasantness for boredom, anger, anxiety, happiness, sadness, and curiosity. When immersed in boredom, participants reported a localized unpleasantness in the head alongside mild bodily deactivation. Linear discriminant analysis confirmed that this somatotopic valence/activity-pattern was distinct from other emotions, supporting the idea that boredom is a unique state with characteristic arousal and valence experiences. Despite this consistent group-level pattern, individual differences emerged with regards to valence sensations in the body, yet these were not linked with boredom proneness and adaptive dealing with boredom. In contrast, these traits variables correlated with distinct body and head activity patterns, particularly among individuals with heightened interoceptive accuracy. Our results underscore the utility of BSM for nuanced mapping of complex emotional states and advance our understanding of how bodily sensations interact with trait factors to shape the subjective experience of boredom.

title: Young carers and educational attainment in late adolescence: a nationally representative analysis of post-16 education in England
authors: Alejandra Letelier, Anne McMunn, Andy McGowan, Krista Cartlidge, Rebecca Lacey
abstract: Introduction: Young carers, defined as individuals <18 years providing unpaid care for someone with disabilities, health conditions, or addiction, are a frequently overlooked group. In the UK, around 12% of young people report caring responsibilities, yet no national studies have examined their post-16 educational attainment outcomes. This study assessed associations between young caring and attainment at Key Stage 5 (age 16-18 years) in England and whether these varied by sociodemographic factors and care characteristics. Methods: Data were drawn from Understanding Society linked to the National Pupil Database in England (2009–2018). The analytic sample included 3,306 adolescents aged 17–18 years. Caring status was self-reported; attainment outcomes were obtained from administrative records. Regression models adjusted for demographic and socioeconomic factors. Additional analyses examined care recipient and care intensity plus modification by gender, ethnicity and socioeconomic position. Results: Young carers had lower educational attainment than their peers. Compared with non-carers, they obtained fewer qualifications (A-Level and equivalent passes, IRR = 0.88, 95% CI 0.79–0.98), were less likely to achieve two or more A-Levels at grades A*–E (OR = 0.67, 95% CI 0.48–0.93) and scored lower on academic and A-Level point totals. In addition, they were also more likely to follow a vocational pathway (OR = 1.40, 95% CI 1.03–1.92). Attainment gaps were larger among boys and higher care intensity (≥10 hours/week). Conclusions: Young carers face persistent educational attainment inequalities at the end of compulsory education, particularly those providing intensive care and boys, highlighting the need for early identification and targeted support.

title: Quantifying the reproductive labor of pregnancy and pregnancy loss in the demographic study of population health
abstract: Pregnancy loss remains a neglected topic within demography, overlooking the significant costs of women’s reproductive labor globally. Using data from Demographic and Health Survey reproductive calendars across 65 countries, this analysis moves beyond live birth to describe the prevalence of pregnancy loss that women across countries experience. The analysis first quantifies the substantial number of person-months that women spend being pregnant in both pregnancies that end in live birth and those that do not. It then calculates a measure of maternal cumulative prevalence of pregnancy loss, demonstrating that women’s experience of pregnancy loss is common worldwide. Next, following the methodology of the Total Fertility Rate (TFR), the analysis calculates a parallel measure called the Total Pregnancy Rate (TPR) to show the average number of pregnancies a woman would experience if she survived through the end of the reproductive years and experienced at each age the period age- specific pregnancy rates. The TPR ranges from 4% to 115% larger than the TFR across countries, with an average of 16% larger. Given that pregnancy loss is known to be undermeasured in many places, these are likely lower bound magnitudes. Finally, cross-country variation in pregnancy loss shows no discernable relationship with potential determinants of pregnancy loss such as abortion incidence rates, per capita health expenditure, rates of anemia prevalence, or TFR, demonstrating the limits of existing fertility frameworks. Counting and considering women’s reproductive labor in the form of pregnancy, whether it ends in a live birth or not, is crucial to understanding women’s and population health, and for gaining a more complete picture of the underlying costs of demographic measures of fertility.

title: The Limits of BPE Tokenization in Polish: Segmentation-Flexional Forms, Grammatical Anchoring, and First-Person Stability in Inflectional Language Models
abstract: This article analyzes BPE tokenization in Polish as a test case for a broader problem: the limits of statistical segmentation in an inflectional language. The starting point is the question of whether, and to what extent, frequency-based tokenization preserves units that are relevant for the linguistic description of Polish: orthographic form, the segmentation-phonemic level, syllable structure, logotomes, prefixes, derivational bases, inflectional endings, grammatical form, and the position of the speaking subject. The empirical material includes the tokenization of diagnostic words, a simple children’s text, selected forms from the Preamble to the Constitution of the Republic of Poland, word-family tests, and examples containing Polish diacritics and nasal vowels. The analysis shows that BPE tokenizers may locally produce segments that coincide with syllabic, logotomic, or morphologically interpretable divisions, but they remain fundamentally dependent on the frequency of written forms. They do not systematically move from orthographic representation to the segmentation-phonemic level or to context-dependent phonetic realization. The results also indicate that BPE stabilizes not grammatical categories themselves, but frequent surface fragments of their exponents. Segments such as -y, -ich, -ni, or -ć may appear as stable tokens or parts of tokens, but this does not amount to preserving the full grammatical form. A form such as ustanawiamy is not merely a sequence ending in -y; it is a verbal form anchored in conjugation, person, number, tense, mood, and aspect. The article develops the concept of grammatical form anchoring, arguing that in Polish a form may not only encode syntactic relations, but also establish the position of the speaking subject. In forms such as poszłam, zrobiłam, or byłam, the grammatical “I” need not be expressed by a separate pronoun, because it is anchored in inflection. In interaction with AI, this reveals an additional problem: a language model does not possess its own stable grammatical “I”, but reconstructs it contextually, often mirroring the user’s forms or shifting grammatical gender. I propose treating Rocławski’s segmentation-flexional forms not as a description of how BPE operates, but as a diagnostic framework for assessing the quality of tokenization boundaries. The findings lead to the research hypothesis that more stable modeling of Polish may require several layers: sublexical stabilization inspired by Rocławski’s system, anchoring grammatical form in the inflectional system, representing sentence patterns and verbal valency structure, and maintaining the position of the grammatical “I” in dialogue. From this perspective, the problem of Polish AI is not reducible to reducing the number of tokens. Rather, it concerns maintaining relations between orthographic representation, word form, grammatical function, sentence position, and the position of the speaking subject.

title: Which Businesses Answer Surveys? Evidence from Dutch Administrative Data
abstract: I leverage a unique administrative register covering the universe of establishments in the Netherlands to examine how characteristics differ between establishments that do and do not respond to business surveys. Only 19% of Dutch establishments responded to regional business surveys in 2022. Responsive establishments employ fewer people, and exhibit higher parttime employment rates, than unresponsive establishments. Sectoral response rates vary by up to 32 percentage points. Enterprises registered to residential addresses comprise over 70% of establishments, yet exhibit response rates 18 percentage points lower than an average office. However, controlling for contact probability reveals that the majority of sectoral and occupational variation in response rates can be traced back to differences in contact probability rather than responsiveness. These findings highlight challenges in the representativeness and generalizability of business survey data, as well as opportunities to improve the design of business surveys.

title: The Effect of a Woman-Friendly Occupation on Employment: U.S. Postmasters Before World War II
abstract: This paper studies U.S. postmasters in the early twentieth century, a rare woman-friendly occupation at a time when women’s employment opportunities were highly constrained. Using a novel dataset linking postmaster appointments to census records, I show that these well-paid, flexible positions attracted educated and qualified White women into the labor force. However, exploiting exogenous variation in reappointment probabilities through an RD design, I find that many women exited the labor force once their appointments ended. Few transitioned into other skilled occupations, accounting for only one-third of the 24 pp. employment loss. The results are consistent with a reservation-wage mechanism: women who secured alternative full-time skilled jobs with wages comparable to postmasters remained employed, while others stopped working. The scarcity of jobs similar to postmasters likely limited the long-term gains of this otherwise advantageous occupation.

title: Governing online hate: The construction of “hateful ideology” in TikTok’s platform governance from 2018 to 2025
abstract: Social media platforms’ understanding of online hate is itself a governance choice with normative and political implications. This study examines the evolution of TikTok’s hate speech governance from 2018 to 2025, focusing on how the platform has conceptualized and deployed the governance concept of “hateful ideology” across its community guidelines, news releases, and transparency reports. Applying longitudinal document analysis (N=132), this study identified two major shifts in the platform’s hate speech policy: a 2020 conceptual shift that restructured hate speech around a four-category framework and a 2023 structural shift that reconfigured policy enforcement around a tiered visibility framework, with “hateful ideology” becoming a central organizing concept. TikTok’s present conception of hateful ideology combines a cognitivist framing of ideology (as belief systems) and a practice-based enforcement logic—a conceptual layering that produces interpretive discretion. The findings suggest that TikTok has primarily configured its concept of “hateful ideology” as a legitimation device calibrated to regulatory contexts, rather than a stable governance principle.

title: Housing Prices and English Period Fertility
abstract: The aim of the study is to produce an estimate of the impact of house prices on English and Welsh period fertility during 2010-23, a period in which there were large declines in period fertility, large changes in house prices and considerable heterogeneity in regional variation of house prices. It uses individual longitudinal data from Understanding Society (the first 14 waves), and it identifies impacts of real house prices on age-specific birth probabilities from variation in changes over time in prices within each region of residence. The estimates imply relatively large negative impacts of real house prices on age-specific birth probabilities. They can be interpreted as causal effects if two conditions hold: (1) there are no omitted time-varying regional variables correlated with house prices and fertility; and (2) women do not move to regions of lower prices in anticipation of future pregnancy. It contributes to the literature by using data on individual women rather than area aggregates. It also employs birth registration data to cross-validate the predictions of the Total Fertility Rate (TFR) and mean age at childbirth from the estimated model.

title: From Bread to Bullets: Sectoral Differences in how Aid Changes the Geography of Conflict
abstract: How does humanitarian aid influence armed conflict dynamics, and how do its effects vary across sectors? This paper analyzes geocoded humanitarian projects and conflict events across Africa (2000–2024) to examine how sector-specific aid shapes territorial control and armed clashes. I argue that aid alters the strategic value of locations through two mechanisms: lootability (material extractability) and visibility (governance signaling). Using matched panel models and spatial grid-cell data, I compare conflict trends across areas with varying levels of aid exposure. Results show that aid increases territorial contestation and clashes. Food and water drive regional violence linked to access competition. Lootable aid produces universal competition, while governance-intensive aid destabilizes rebel-held zones. Violence against aid workers remains limited and largely opportunistic.

title: The Cooperative Scope of Local Gods: Punishment, Omniscience, and Distant Cooperation among the Sihanaka of Madagascar
authors: Anders Norge, Daniel Major-Smith, Benjamin Grant Purzycki
abstract: Do supernatural beliefs in punishment and omniscience promote cooperation among distant unrelated individuals? Previous cross-cultural research – predominantly among “moralistic” religious traditions – suggests it might, although numerous open questions remain. Foremost among these are: “Do these results replicate among local religious traditions?” and “Is the relationship causal?” Here, we take a causally-informed approach to explore these questions among the Sihanaka from Madagascar. Our research question is: Do punishment and omniscience beliefs about a local god - zazavavindrano, an aquatic deity - promote cooperation towards co-religionists of this tradition? We assessed cooperation using Random Allocation and Dictator Games. Bayesian regression models were used, adjusting for plausible confounding factors, with g-computation methods to estimate our counterfactual causal effects of interest (i.e., the joint effect of zazavavindrano punishment and omniscience beliefs on cooperation with distant co-religionists). Across all games, conditions, and confounding assumptions, we found little evidence for a relationship between these zazavavindrano beliefs and cooperation. While zazavavindrano beliefs may not promote cooperation with unknown distant co-religionists, whether these beliefs facilitate cooperation in other domains – such as ecological management of wetlands, which is of greater concern to zazavavindrano than moral behaviour towards strangers – is an interesting open question for future research.

title: An Astonishing Turn in the Attribution Debate on the Law of Comparative Advantage
abstract: The law of comparative advantage cannot be attributed to David Ricardo, James Mill, Robert Torrens or John Stuart Mill. Their writings, much like those of Adam Smith, strictly adhere to the classical rule for specialization, which asserts that one should acquire commodities abroad whenever offered cheaper than it would cost to produce them locally. They all viewed the cheaper price of foreign commodities as the logical starting point and condicio sine qua non of most exchanges between countries. Furthermore, the popular narrative about Ricardo’s alleged formulation of an alternative rule for specialization – the law or principle of comparative advantage – results from a misinterpretation of the well-known numerical example presented in Chapter 7 of his Principles of Political Economy and Taxation. John Stuart Mill is largely responsible for this misinterpretation; he obviously deserves no recognition or praise for that.

title: Illicit Financial Outflows and Domestic Linkage Disruption in Nigeria
abstract: Illicit financial outflows remain a major constraint on Nigeria’s development, yet much of the literature focuses on their causes and scale rather than their effects on domestic economic linkages. This paper examines illicit financial outflows through the capital flight channel, using residual-method capital flight estimates as an empirical proxy for unrecorded outward resource movements. The paper develops an Economic Linkages Framework to explain how capital flight disrupts the channels through which domestic investment generates multiplier effects, employment, fiscal capacity, supply-chain deepening, and productivity spillovers. Drawing on Hirschman’s (1958) linkage theory, Keynesian multiplier analysis, and endogenous growth theory, it introduces a nine-type typology of domestic linkages covering production, financial, fiscal, labour market, consumption, technology, trade-external, institutional, and spatial-territorial dimensions. The empirical analysis is illustrative rather than causal, given the small sample of 26 annual observations. OLS results show that unemployment is strongly and negatively associated with GDP growth (R² = 0.574), suggesting that the labour market channel is the most visible linkage pathway in the available data. The direct and squared capital flight terms are not statistically significant, so the non-linear linkage disruption hypothesis remains suggestive rather than confirmed. The paper contributes a conceptual framework for analysing illicit financial outflows as a process of domestic linkage disruption and structural fragmentation, with implications for capital retention, employment, fiscal capacity, and institutional reform in Nigeria and comparable resource-dependent economies.

title: Does Carbon Pricing Outperform Command-and-Control Regulation? Firm-Level Evidence from Korea’s Dual Regulatory Framework
abstract: This study exploits South Korea's unique dual-policy framework to evaluate the comparative effects of carbon pricing and command-and-control regulation on firm-level environmental performance. Using a difference-in-differences design with firm-level panel data from 2011 to 2022, I compare outcomes between firms regulated under a command-and-control program (Target Management System, TMS) and those subject to a market-based carbon pricing mechanism (Emissions Trading Scheme, ETS). The results show that ETS-regulated firms reduced energy use by approximately 5.8% to 8.8% and carbon emissions by 7.3% to 8.5% across model specifications. However, the effects on carbon intensity were inconsistent. Event-study analyses suggest that these differing effects are driven by the heterogeneous timing of firm responses: immediate but short-lived reductions in energy use, persistent declines in carbon emissions, and gradual improvements in emissions efficiency. Phase-specific estimates further indicate that more market-oriented ETS phases were associated with stronger reductions in carbon emissions and intensity, underscoring the role of incentive-based policy design in enhancing environmental outcomes.

title: Reverse-engineering is not enough for inter-group transmission of novel palaeolithic knapping techniques
authors: Vincent Niochet, Morgan Roussel, Krist Vaesen, Alexander Verpoorte, Marie Soressi
abstract: Identifying evidence of cultural transmission from archaeological artefacts is essential for reconstructing the dynamics of social interactions during prehistory. Although technological similarities among overlapping archaeological cultures are often interpreted as indicators of direct contact, the degree to which social interaction is necessary for the diffusion of knapping technologies remains debated. Transmission of technologies might, in some cases, result from the reverse-engineering of discarded stone artefacts. Here, we experimentally tested the library of stones hypothesis, which posits that even an unfamiliar knapping technique can be identified solely through observation of its resulting artefacts. To evaluate this, 34 experienced knappers participated in an experiment in which they were asked to identify the knapping technique used to produce three experimental sets of stone artefacts, each made with a distinct technique. Participants generally failed to reverse-engineer techniques with which they were unfamiliar. Successful reverse-engineering was primarily driven by prior practical experience of the technique and diversity of technological repertoire. These results suggest that the cultural transmission of technological innovations likely required direct social learning mechanisms to occur between groups. Experimental approaches of this kind offer valuable insights into the processes underlying cultural transmission and provide a promising framework for reconstructing inter-group interaction during prehistory.

title: Better futures: Supported accommodation for unaccompanied children and young people experiencing homelessness
authors: Catherine Robinson, Carmel Hobbs, Deborah Batterham, Joel McGregor, Wendy Stone, Megan Robinson
abstract: This research project examines the effectiveness of Australia’s supported accommodation services in meeting the needs of unaccompanied children and young people aged 12–24. It proposes principles to guide policy and practice toward a better system. Unaccompanied children and young people experiencing homelessness are at risk of significant lifelong negative outcomes. Supported accommodation services are a critical response to homelessness; however, these services do not always meet this cohort’s needs. A strengthened system of care is required to better align services to unaccompanied young people’s needs.

title: From Lombroso to Cognitive Immunity: Integrating Classical Criminological Theory with the Integrated Bio-Ethical Literacy (IBL) Model — The Impulse Gap, Deterrence Deficit, and Regulatory Realism as Theoretical Contributions to the Primary Prevention of Child Sexual Offences
abstract: Classical criminology has, over a century and a half, produced increasingly sophisticated explanations of why human beings commit acts of sexual violence against children — from Lombroso's constitutional positivism to Sutherland's sociology of learned conduct to Bandura's account of moral disengagement. Each stage has advanced the field. None has produced a systematic, evidence-based architecture for primary prevention: a mechanism for rendering such offending structurally less probable before it occurs. That is the gap this paper addresses. Three theoretical constructs are advanced here — the Impulse Gap, the Deterrence Deficit, and Regulatory Realism — each grounded in existing criminological literature but applied, for the first time as a synthesized framework, to the specific preventive challenge posed by child sexual offending in India. The Impulse Gap identifies the explanatory space within Sutherland's Differential Association framework — partially addressed but not resolved by Gottfredson and Hirschi's self-control theory — where opportunistic, neurobiologically driven offending escapes both social learning explanation and rational-actor deterrence.
